items.foco: Escherichia coli causes more urinary tract infections
items.title: E. coli in UTI
items.contentHtml:
E. coli in UTI

  • Escherichia coli is the first cause of uncomplicated urinary tract infection (>80% of cases).
  • The main virulence factor is P fimbriae (adhesins that bind to uroepithelial receptors).
  • In acute pyelonephritis, the diagnosis is confirmed with positive urine culture. (>105 CFU/mL).
  • First empirical line: trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (if resistance <20%) or nitrofurantoin in cystitis.
  • Trap: strains producing ESBL (extended-spectrum beta-lactamases) are resistant to cephalosporins. 3rd generation; treat with ertapenem or fosfomycin trometamol.

items.title: KPC: K. pneumoniae carbapenemase
items.contentHtml:
KPC: K. pneumoniae carbapenemase

  • Carbapenemase-producing Klebsiella pneumoniae (KPC) is a cause of nosocomial infection with high mortality (40-50%).
  • Mechanism: enzyme carbapenemase type A (serine-betalactamase) that hydrolyzes carbapenems and almost all beta-lactams.
  • Diagnosis: modified Hodge test or PCR for blaKPC gene.
  • First line therapy: combination of ceftazidime-avibactam (dose: 2.5 g every 8 h intravenous).
  • Trap: if the strain produces metallo-betalactamase (NDM), ceftazidime-avibactam is not active; use ceftazidime-avibactam + aztreonam or tigecycline.

items.title: Typhoid fever due to S. typhi
items.contentHtml:
Typhoid fever due to S. typhi

  • Salmonella typhi causes typhoid fever, prolonged fever, relative bradycardia and pink spots on the trunk.
  • Physiopathology: invasion of Peyer's patches in the distal ileum; can cause intestinal perforation (surgical emergency).
  • Diagnosis: blood culture (positive in the first week), stool culture (2nd-3rd week), Widal reaction (antibodies O and H, although with false positives).
  • First line: ceftriaxone (2 g/day intravenous) or azithromycin (500 mg/day PO) due to resistance to chloramphenicol.
  • Trap: chronic carrier (gallbladder) is treated with ciprofloxacin 750 mg every 12 h PO for 4-6 weeks.

items.foco: Shigella causes dysentery by mucosal invasion
items.title: Shigella and invasive dysentery
items.contentHtml:
Shigella and invasive dysentery

  • Shigella (species: flexneri, sonnei, dysenteriae, boydii) causes bacillary dysentery by invasion of the colonic mucosa.
  • Pathophysiology: secretion of Shiga toxin (similar to EHEC) that causes epithelial necrosis and diarrhea with blood, mucus and tenesmus.
  • Diagnosis: stool culture on SS (Salmonella-Shigella) medium and serological agglutination.
  • First line: azithromycin (500 mg/day PO for 3 days) or ciprofloxacin (500 mg every 12 h PO for 3 days).
  • Trap: do not use antidiarrheals (loperamide) because they retain the pathogen and increase the risk of toxic megacolon.

items.title: Enterobacter cloacae AmpC
items.contentHtml:
Enterobacter cloacae AmpC

  • Enterobacter cloacae has inducible AmpC beta-lactamase encoded in the chromosome; exposure to 3rd generation cephalosporins selects derepressed mutants.
  • Resistance: to ceftriaxone, cefotaxime and ceftazidime; they do not hydrolyze cefepime or carbapenems (in strains without other beta-lactamases).
  • Diagnosis: induction test with cefoxitin (adjacent cefoxitin and ceftazidime disk; synergy suggests AmpC).
  • First line: cefepime (2 g every 8 h intravenous) or carbapenem if infection serious.
  • Trap: in catheter infection or bacteremia, consider device removal and antibiotic adjustment due to sensitivity.

items.title: Y. enterocolitica and adenitis
items.contentHtml:
And. enterocolitica and adenitis

  • Yersinia enterocolitica causes mesenteric adenitis (pseudoappendicitis) with pain in the right iliac fossa, fever and diarrhea.
  • Pathophysiology: invasion of mesenteric lymph nodes; can produce erythema nodosum and reactive arthritis (post-infectious).
  • Diagnosis: stool culture in CIN (cefsulodin-irgasan-novobiocin) medium at 25-30°C; serology for anti-Yersinia antibodies.
  • First line: trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (800/160 mg every 12 h PO) or ciprofloxacin in severe cases.
  • Trap: differentiate from acute appendicitis; if there is mesenteric adenitis, avoid unnecessary surgery; The treatment is medical.

items.foco: Proteus mirabilis produces urease and stones
items.title: Proteus mirabilis and lithiasis
items.contentHtml:
Proteus mirabilis and lithiasis

  • Proteus mirabilis produces urease that hydrolyzes urea to ammonium, alkalinizing the urine (pH >7).
  • Alkalinization precipitates magnesium ammonium phosphate (struvite), forming coral-shaped stones (staghorn).
  • Pathophysiology: the stones act as a bacterial nest, perpetuating the infection; require percutaneous nephrolithotomy for complete eradication.
  • First line: ciprofloxacin (500 mg every 12 h PO) or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole if sensitive; associate acetohydroxamic acid (urease inhibitor, second line).
  • Trap: Proteus mirabilis is intrinsically resistant to tetracycline and colistin.

items.foco: Serratia marcescens associated with nosocomial infections
items.title: Serratia marcescens nosocomial
items.contentHtml:
Nosocomial Serratia marcescens

  • Serratia marcescens is an opportunistic nosocomial pathogen, common in intensive care unit and patients with catheters or mechanical ventilation.
  • It produces urinary tract infections, pneumonia and bacteremia; has inducible AmpC beta-lactamase and resistance to multiple antibiotics.
  • Virulence factor: prodigiosin (red pigment) and proteases that degrade IgA.
  • First line: cefepime (2 g every 8 h intravenous) or carbapenem (meropenem 1 g every 8 h intravenous).
  • Trap: Serratia is resistant to polymyxin B and colistin by LPS modification; Do not use these drugs.

items.title: Citrobacter freundii AmpC
items.contentHtml:
Citrobacter freundii AmpC

  • Citrobacter freundii possesses inducible chromosomal AmpC beta-lactamase, similar to Enterobacter; it is derepressed with exposure to 3rd generation cephalosporins.
  • Resistance: to ceftriaxone, cefotaxime and ceftazidime; remains sensitive to cefepime and carbapenems.
  • Diagnosis: induction test with cefoxitin (positive if there is synergy between cefoxitin disc and ceftazidime).
  • First line: cefepime (2 g every 8 h intravenous) or meropenem (1 g every 8 h intravenous) in severe infections.
  • Trap: Citrobacter freundii can acquire ESBL or carbapenemases through plasmids; request extended antibiogram.

items.title: Morganella morganii and colistin
items.contentHtml:
Morganella morganii and colistin

  • Morganella morganii is a facultative anaerobic gram-negative bacillus that presents intrinsic resistance to colistin and polymyxin B.
  • Mechanism: modification of lipid A of LPS by ArnT enzymes and PmrC, reducing the negative charge.
  • Frequently causes urinary infection and wound infection in immunocompromised patients.
  • First line: carbapenem (meropenem 1 g every 8 h intravenous) or cefepime (2 g every 8 h intravenous), depending sensitivity.
  • Trap: do not use colistin or tigecycline (natural resistance); Morganella is a producer of urease, but not as potent as Proteus.
